one_line_takeaway: VertMark embeds an owner-identity-bound backdoor watermark into vertical-domain PLMs in seconds via training-free word-embedding replacement, achieving stronger black-box verification accuracy and far lower embedding cost than four prior backdoor-watermarking baselines (POR, PLMmark, BadEdit, PTLayer) across medical/financial/legal NLU and NLG tasks, though its NLG watermark still fails to survive INT4 quantization.
key_finding: VertMark embeds in ~0.003 hours (~11s) versus BadEdit's 0.016-0.063h, PTLayer's 2.4-47h, and PLMmark's 3.1-15.9h, while matching or beating original-model F1 on most tasks (e.g., NLU: 93.61/85.52/40.29/83.13/92.72/71.32/76.04 F1 across NER/RE/QA/FSA/NTC/LLTC/LMC vs. Original 93.61/85.52/40.29/83.30/92.69/71.28/76.00). VertMark achieved WACC >80% on all NLU tasks (e.g., 94.07 NER, 90.64 RE, 87.14 QA) and >85% on all NLG tasks, generally exceeding BadEdit (which fell as low as 1.96% WACC on financial FSA) while keeping FPR low (0.00-21.80%, though FPR reached 14.05% on medical NER and 18.29% on legal LLTC, higher than some baselines on those specific tasks). Under INT4 quantization, all methods including VertMark collapsed to near-0% WACC on NLG tasks, though VertMark retained higher NLU WACC (e.g., 63.11% medical, 76.53% finance) than most baselines. Under model-fusion removal, baselines' WACC often dropped to near-zero (e.g., PTLayer 68.00%->0.00% medical NLU) while VertMark degraded more gracefully (90.64%->70.29% medical NLU). Under the paper's own adaptive attack (assuming attacker has full knowledge of VertMark's mechanism), embedding re-initialization could drive WACC to 0% but only by also destroying task F1 (e.g., legal NLG F1 dropped 92.92->13.81), making it an impractical attack in the authors' framing; a milder global linear transformation left WACC largely intact (e.g., medical NLU WACC actually rose 90.64->92.00). Extraction/distillation experiments (medical domain only) showed VertMark's WACC exceeding 70% after 5 training epochs on a 2GB general corpus, whereas baseline trigger mechanisms reportedly failed to transfer into the student model under distillation.
core_contribution: VertMark, a training-free black-box backdoor watermarking framework for copyright protection of vertical-domain pre-trained language models (VPLMs) in medical, financial, and legal domains. Its own novel contributions (as distinct from cited prior work) are: (1) generalizing a previously single-domain (medical-only) training-free watermarking mechanism from the authors' own prior MM'25 paper [19] into a unified framework applicable across arbitrary vertical domains; (2) a watermark embedding method that directly replaces the word-embedding vector of a low-frequency 'trigger' token with a linearly-scaled, Gaussian-noised copy of a high-frequency domain-relevant 'replacement' token's embedding -- no gradient-based training required, taking only ~0.003 hours (about 11 seconds) per embedding; (3) an owner-identity-bound trigger selection scheme that derives trigger-token indices deterministically via RSA signing (S(*)) of a message m_i = s||K_priv||i followed by SHA-256 hashing, mapped modulo the candidate low-frequency token set size; (4) task-specific verification protocols -- a sensitivity-based sample-filtering step for NLU/classification tasks (removing verification samples whose prediction does not change under a synonym substitution near the trigger) and a semantic-similarity verification (Sentence-BERT / all-MiniLM-L6-v2 cosine similarity, thresholded via ROC/Youden-index-optimal gamma) for NLG/generation tasks.
threat_model: Model owner trains a VPLM on costly domain-specific corpora, embeds a watermark, and deploys it via an MLaaS API. An attacker steals the watermarked model, applies watermark-removal operations (pruning, quantization, model extraction/distillation, model-fusion-based backdoor removal, output rewriting/paraphrasing, or an adaptive attack that assumes full knowledge of VertMark's mechanism), fine-tunes it on downstream tasks, and redeploys it as a commercial API without revealing internal parameters. The defender/verifier has only black-box query access to the suspicious API (no weights, no gradients) and must produce ownership evidence by submitting paired queries (one containing a replacement token, one with the token swapped for its corresponding trigger token) and checking output consistency. Attacker is not assumed to have access to the owner's high-quality domain corpus (extraction attacks are simulated instead with a 2GB general-domain corpus) or to the private signing key needed to reconstruct the trigger set.
limitations: The paper's own robustness results show real gaps rather than uniform success: INT4 quantization destroys NLG watermark detectability for every method tested including VertMark (0.00% WACC across all three NLG domains), meaning the framework's claimed quantization robustness holds only for NLU tasks and only up to INT8. FPR is not uniformly low -- it reaches 14.05% (medical NER) and 18.29% (legal LLTC), which is worse than some baselines on those same tasks (e.g., BadEdit's FPR is lower on medical NER at 9.36%... though VertMark's is 14.05%, higher). The model-extraction/distillation robustness claim rests on a single medical-domain case study using a small 2GB corpus, explicitly justified by 'high computational cost' rather than broader validation across all three domains and both task types. The adaptive-attack analysis is limited to two coarse global perturbations (linear transform, re-init) chosen because the authors argue 'precise removal is difficult' -- this is a claim, not a proof, and a more targeted or optimization-based adaptive attacker is not evaluated. The mechanism itself is explicitly described as an extension/generalization of the authors' own prior single-domain (medical-only) watermarking paper [19] (MM'25), so the core trigger-replacement idea is not new to this paper -- the contribution is the cross-domain unification and the NLU/NLG-specific verification protocols layered on top. Trigger token count is fixed at n=8 by default, described as an empirically chosen balance rather than derived from a principled bound, and the paper notes larger n could aid coverage but was not systematically explored beyond this default in the main results.

datasets_used: Legalbench-Instruct (Legal QA and Legal Causal Reasoning, legal NLG)
quantitative_results: VertMark embedding time: 0.003 hours (~11s) across all tasks, vs. BadEdit 0.016-0.063h, PTLayer 2.4-47.0h, PLMmark 3.1-15.9h, POR 1.2-3.4h
VertMark WACC (NLU): 94.07% NER, 90.64% RE, 87.14% QA (medical); 93.51% FSA, 86.05% NTC (financial); 87.00% LLTC, 80.48% LMC (legal)
VertMark WACC (NLG): 85.00% Medical QA, 93.30% MDA; 91.00% Financial QA, 93.00% SP; 92.00% Legal QA, 86.33% LCR
VertMark F1 vs Original F1 (NLU, medical NER): 93.61 vs 93.61 (identical); (financial FSA): 83.13 vs 83.30
Under INT4 quantization, VertMark NLG WACC drops to 0.00% in all three domains (medical, financial, legal), matching the collapse seen in BadEdit and PTLayer
Under model-fusion removal attack, VertMark WACC drops from 90.64% to 70.29% (medical NLU) vs. PTLayer's collapse from 68.00% to 0.00%
Under adaptive embedding re-initialization attack, VertMark WACC drops to 0.00% but downstream F1 also collapses (e.g., legal NLG F1 92.92 -> 13.81)
Under adaptive linear-transformation attack, VertMark WACC is largely preserved or even increases (e.g., medical NLU WACC 90.64% -> 92.00%)
Under rewriting attack (Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct paraphrasing), VertMark WACC drops only modestly (e.g., 91.00% -> 63.00% financial QA) vs. PTLayer's 98.00% -> 6.00% and BadEdit's 75.03% -> 0.96%
Model extraction (distillation, medical domain, 2GB general corpus): VertMark WACC exceeds 70% after 5 training epochs

Default hyperparameters: n=8 trigger tokens, scaling factor lambda=1.5, Gaussian noise mu=0.1, sigma^2=0.01, low-frequency trigger token band 0.001%-0.01% occurrence probability, 10 generated verification samples per replacement token
baselines_compared: PTLayer (passthrough layers keyed to a private key)
